coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of NS-doped carbon dots as sensitive “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ and GSH detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Huan
Mu, Wencheng
Wang, Siying
Shi, Lin
Ma, Tianfeng
Lu, Yongchang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) were successfully prepared by a dehydration exothermic carbonization method. The NS-CQDs exhibited uniform size distribution, splendid photostability, and bright fluorescence emission with a fluorescence quantum yield of 24.1 %. It was found that Cu2+ could quench the fluorescence at 467 nm based on the static quenching effect when Cu2+ was added to the NS-CQDs. At this time, the fluorescence sensor changed from the “ON” state to the “OFF” state. When glutathione (GSH) was further introduced into the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was amazingly restored through the coordination reaction between GSH and Cu2+. The fluorescence sensor changed from the “OFF” state to the “ON” state. Therefore, NS-CQDs as an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescence sensor was designed for sequential detection of Cu2+ and GSH. Furthermore, this study successfully demonstrated the sensor’s ability to selectively detect Cu2+ and GSH within a wide concentration range. Specifically, the detection range for Cu2+ was 0.1 μM−200.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.07 μM, while the range for GSH was 0.6 μM−180.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.1 μM. Most importantly, the NS-CQDs nanosensor could reliably monitor Cu2+ and GSH levels in human serum samples, with significant potential for practical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: NS-CQDs
Fluorescence
Cu2+
GSH
ON-OFF-ON

TEM and XRD characterization of NS-CQDs
XPS and FT-IR analysis of NS-CQDs
Determination of Cu2+ by the NS-CQDs
Determination of GSH by the NS-CQDs/Cu2+

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province, China (Grant No.2019-ZJ-944Q); the Phytochemistry Key Laboratory of Tibetan Plateau of Qinghai Province, China; the Provincial Thousand Talents Program of High-level Innovation Talent, China; the Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Tibetan Medicine Resources on Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, China; the Qinghai Minzu University Postgraduate Innovation Project, China; the University-level Planning Project of Qinghai Nationality University of Qinghai Province, China (Grant No.2021XJGH16); the Innovation Team of Medicinal Material Resource Protection and High Value Utilization in Qinghai Province, China. (Grant No.2021XJPI02).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the NS-CQDs at 20 nm scale; (B) HRTEM image of the NS-CQDs lattice spacing; (C) The histograms of particle size distribution of the NS-CQDs; (D) XRD spectrum of the NS-CQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption, optimal excitation and emission spectra of NS-CQDs (Inset: images of NS-CQDs under sunlight and 365 nm UV light); (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of NS-CQDs at various excitation wavelengths (340–440 nm).
(A) XPS spectra of NS-CQDs; (B) The high-resolution XPS spectra of S 2p, (C) C 1s, (D) N 1s, and (E) O 1s; (F) Atomic percentage of elements in NS-CQDs.
Fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs at different time of irradiation with xenon lamps (A), at different storage time (B), and at different concentrations of NaCl solutions (C).

Fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs in different buffer solutions (A); different pH (B); different incubation time between NS-CQDs and Cu2+ (C); different incubation time between NS-CQDs/Cu2+ and GSH (D).

(A) Selectivity of NS-CQDs for Cu2+; (B) Anti-interference property of NS-CQDs/Cu2+; (C) The fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs with 0.1–200.0 μM Cu2+; (D) The fitted line relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of Cu2+.

(A) Selectivity of NS-CQDs/Cu2+ for GSH; (B) Anti-interference property of NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH; (C) The fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs/Cu2+ with 0.6–180 μM GSH; (D) The fitted line relationship between (F1-F)/F and the concentration of GSH.

(A) TEM image of NS-CQDs/Cu2+; (B) TEM image of NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH; (C) The dependence of the NS-CQDs fluorescence intensity on the concentration of Cu2+; (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of NS-CQDs, NS-CQDs/Cu2+, and NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH; (E) The fluorescence lifetime of NS-CQDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+.
Schematic of synthesized NS-CQDs for Cu2+ and GSH detection.
Determination of Cu2+ by the NS-CQDs in actual serum samples (n = 3).
Determination of GSH by the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ in actual serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of NS-doped carbon dots as sensitive “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor for Cu2+ and GSH detection

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Huan Wang and Wencheng Mu are joint first authors.
These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) were successfully prepared by a dehydration exothermic carbonization method. The NS-CQDs exhibited uniform size distribution, splendid photostability, and bright fluorescence emission with a fluorescence quantum yield of 24.1 %. It was found that Cu2+ could quench the fluorescence at 467 nm based on the static quenching effect when Cu2+ was added to the NS-CQDs. At this time, the fluorescence sensor changed from the “ON” state to the “OFF” state. When glutathione (GSH) was further introduced into the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was amazingly restored through the coordination reaction between GSH and Cu2+. The fluorescence sensor changed from the “OFF” state to the “ON” state. Therefore, NS-CQDs as an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescence sensor was designed for sequential detection of Cu2+ and GSH. Furthermore, this study successfully demonstrated the sensor’s ability to selectively detect Cu2+ and GSH within a wide concentration range. Specifically, the detection range for Cu2+ was 0.1 μM−200.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.07 μM, while the range for GSH was 0.6 μM−180.0 μM with a detection limit of 0.1 μM. Most importantly, the NS-CQDs nanosensor could reliably monitor Cu2+ and GSH levels in human serum samples, with significant potential for practical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are an emerging class of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials with diameters less than 10 nm [1], which have unique properties such as stable photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, and tunable fluorescence emission [2,3]. Compared with metal nanoclusters and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), CQDs are considered promising nanomaterials in many fields, such as drug delivery [4,5], bioimaging [6,7], biosensing [8], and photodynamic therapy [9]. In 2004, Xu et al. discovered CQDs for the first time during the chemical oxidation of single-walled carbon nanotubes with nitric acid [10]. In 2014, Chen and Zhang efficiently used N-CQDs as a fluorescent probe to sensitively detect Hg2+ [11]. In 2018, Li's group successfully prepared P-CQDs by hydrothermal method, and a sensing system was designed for Cu2+ detection [12]. In 2023, Song et al. successfully synthesized S-CQDs as fluorescent sensor for detecting Fe3+ [13]. In recent years, various elements, including nitrogen (N), boron (B), chlorine (Cl), sulfur (S), and phosphorus (P), have been utilized as dopants for CQDs [14]. In the context of these advancements, more people are opting for N and S as co-doping agents to engineer distinct material properties within CQDs. The co-dope N and S offers several significant advantages compared to singly-doped N or S carbon dots, including enhanced fluorescence emission, increased sensitivity, and the capability to adjust the optical and electronic characteristics of the CQDs. The enhancement of fluorescence emission can be attributed to the synergistic effect of N and S co-doping, which greatly improves the photoluminescent performance of CQDs [15]. The increased sensitivity of CQDs co-doped with N and S to various analytes renders them especially valuable in fluorescence sensing applications, particularly in detecting low concentrations of target molecules or ions [16]. Moreover, the tunability resulting from adjusting the N and S doping ratios enables further fine-tuning CQDs' optical and electronic properties, facilitating customization for specific sensing applications [17,18]. Therefore, this study is dedicated to exploring the synthesis of NS-CQDs for further advancements in fluorescence sensing research.

It is of significance to determine the levels of heavy transition metal ions, such as Ag+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, as they pollute the environment and have toxic effects on living organisms [19,20]. Despite copper being considered a crucial trace element for different metabolic processes, excessive accumulation of copper might cause health problems for humans [21,22]. As a result, it is essential to develop novel methods for the effective and efficient detection of Cu2+.

It is well known that there is a specific binding ability between copper ions and glutathione [23]. They are often used as an active molecule pair in signal switching-sensing systems. Glutathione (GSH), as a thiolated tripeptide, represents the most abundant bioethanol in cells and is also one of the most potent antioxidants [24]. In spite of this, a low level of GSH has been linked to a number of diseases, including Alzheimer's disease, cardiovascular disease, and cancer [25–27]. Thus, detection of the GSH level in biological systems was crucial to accurately diagnosing the disease and determining the severity of the problem. So far, there are various analytical methods available for the detection of GSH, including high performance liquid chromatography [28], mass spectrometry [29], colorimetry [30], electrochemistry [31], and fluorescence spectroscopy [32]. Among these methods, fluorescence spectroscopy is favored for its simplicity of operation, wide measurement range, and fast analysis. Therefore, a simple, fast, and effective fluorescence sensor will be required to analyze GSH in living organisms.

In many analytical tests, the detection principle of CQDs as a fluorescence sensor is the “TURN-OFF” or “TURN-ON” process of CQDs emitting fluorescence to the analytes, which corresponds to the fluorescence quenching or recovery. Compared with the single OFF/ON mode, fluorescent probes operating in the “ON-OFF-ON” mode have higher reproducibility and can effectively avoid false positive signals [33]. Therefore, developing a novel “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensor will have broader prospects in detection and analysis.

In this study, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NS-CQDs) were synthesized by a dehydration exothermic carbonization method using glucose (Glu), ethylenediamine (EDA), and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) as raw materials. It was remarkable that the maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of NS-CQDs was 467 nm when the excitation wavelength was 390 nm. When Cu2+ was introduced into the NS-CQDs solution, the fluorescence of NS-CQDs was quenched due to the static quenching effect. Furthermore, the continuous introduction of GSH into the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system resulted in a significant fluorescence recovery of NS-CQDs. This phenomenon indicated that the coordination between Cu2+ and GSH was more robust than the interaction between Cu2+ and NS-CQDs. Therefore, an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent nanosensor was successfully constructed based on NS-CQDs, which operating principle can be seen in Scheme 1 . Compared with various previously reported detection methods, the designed fluorescence sensor exhibited simplicity, sensitivity, and efficiency. Moreover, the sensor has excellent performance for detecting Cu2+ and GSH in human serum samples.

All reagents were of analytical reagent grade and used without further purification. Glucose (Glu), maltose (Mal), sucrose (Suc), ethylenediamine (EDA), glutathione(GSH), oxidized glutathione (GSSG), concentrated sulphuric acid, Tris, CH3COOH, HCl, H3PO4, H3BO3, KCl, NaOH, Na2HPO4, NaH2PO4, NaCl, Na2CO3, and NaHCO3 was purchased from Tianjin Hongyan Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate, L-alanine (L-Ala), L-cysteine (L-Cys), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), L-proline (L-Pro), L-threonine (L-Thr), L-histidine (L-His), L-methionine (L-Met), L-lysine (L-Lys), L-serine (L-Ser), Glycine(Gly), ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), salicylic acid (SA), thioethylamine (TEA), and thioglycolic acid (TGA) were both purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The preparation reagent of metal ion solutions were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shenyang, China).

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained with a JEM-2100F microscope (JEOL, Japan). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on Bruker D2 Phaser diffractometer. Fluorescence spectra were measured by RF-5301-PC Luminescence Spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum was recorded on the TU-1901 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Beijing General Instrument Co., Ltd., Beijing, China). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on an ESCALAB250Xi instrument. Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) were measured via IR Prestige21 spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Ltd., Tokyo, Japan). The pH of the buffer solution was determined by a pHS-2C digital pH meter (Ridao Science Instrument Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China).

By a dehydration exothermic carbonization method, NS-CQDs were synthesized from glucose (Glu), ethylenediamine (EDA), and concentrated sulfuric acid. First, 0.5 g of Glu and 15 mL of EDA were completely dissolved in 35 mL of ultrapure water via sonication and vortexing. As soon as 10 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid was added to the above mixed solution, a lot of white smoke and heat were released. After cooling to room temperature, a dark brown solution was obtained. Subsequently, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and then a 0.22 μm filter was used to remove larger particles. As a result of freeze-drying the solution, NS-CQDs solid powder was prepared. Finally, the powder was dissolved in ultrapure water and then stored in a refrigerator (4 ℃) for further application and analysis.

The fluorescence quantum yield of NS-CQDs was calculated using quinine sulfate dissolved in a 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution (QY = 54.0%) as a reference [34]. The calculation followed the formula described below:
Q
Y s
= Q
Y
s t
η
s
2
η
s t
2
I s
A s
A
s t
I
s t

The formula incorporates various variables, namely the quantum yield (represented as QY), the refractive index of the solvent (denoted as η), the integrated fluorescence intensity (represented by I), the absorbance (denoted as A), and the physical quantities of NS-CQDs and quinine sulfate (represented as s and st, respectively). Moreover, the absorbance values and corresponding integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 390 nm) of quinine sulfate and NS-CQDs solutions were measured at different concentrations, respectively. The UV absorbance value below 0.1 was recorded to minimize the reabsorption effect.

NS-CQDs were utilized as fluorescent probes to detect Cu2+ and GSH. The experiment was conducted in a 0.1 M KH2PO4-NaOH buffer solution (PDP-NaOH) with a pH of 7, providing an ideal environment for the sensing process. During the initial phase, the fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs with various concentrations of Cu2+ were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. To construct the fluorescence sensing platform NS-CQDs/Cu2+, 30 μL of NS-CQDs (1.3 mg·mL−1) was mixed with 2 mL of PDP-NaOH (0.1 M) and 50 μL of Cu2+ (200.0 μM). Subsequently, different concentrations of GSH were added to the sensing system, and the resulting fluorescence spectra were recorded at 390 nm excitation wavelength.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and GSH in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The human blood used for actual sample analysis was obtained from the Fourth People's Hospital of Qinghai Province. Fresh human blood was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to ensure accurate results. Subsequently, the supernatant was diluted 1,000 times with ultrapure water to obtain pure serum samples. The diluted serum samples were added with different concentrations of Cu2+ and GSH solution. Moreover, the spiked samples were analyzed using the same procedures as detecting Cu2+ and GSH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TEM and XRD characterization of NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized NS-CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) to prove the successful preparation of NS-CQDs. The TEM image displayed the uniform morphology and excellent monodispersity of the prepared NS-CQDs (Fig. 1 A). As shown in Fig. 1C, the size distribution of the synthesized NS-CQDs was researched, revealing the particle size range of 1–5 nm and the average particle size of 3.3 nm. The HRTEM image in Fig. 1B showed 0.22 nm lattice spacing, which confirmed that the NS-CQDs were crystalline graphite structure. In addition, an intense diffraction peak at 2θ = 20.57° emerged in the XRD pattern (Fig. 1D), corresponding to the (002) plane of the graphite structure and suggesting the presence of highly disordered carbon atoms [35]. According to the above results, the synthesized NS-CQDs exhibited highly crystalline and graphitic structures.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra were utilized to analyze the optical properties of the synthesized NS-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the UV–vis absorption spectra revealed a maximum characteristic absorption wavelength at 313 nm in the absorption band of 220∼650 nm, which may be ascribable to the π-π* or n-π* transition of carbon nanomaterials [36]. Further exploration of fluorescence properties displayed optimum excitation (Ex) and maximum emission (Em) wavelengths at 390 nm and 467 nm, respectively. Notably, the excitation and emission spectra exhibited mirror symmetry, consistent with fluorescent carbon nanomaterials. The NS-CQDs solution exhibited brown under visible light while intriguingly showing blue fluorescence under ultraviolet irradiation at 365 nm (Fig. 2A inset). The fluorescence behavior could be attributed to the size and surface defects of the synthesized NS-CQDs fluorescent nanoprobes. Additionally, the excitation wavelength dependence of the prepared NS-CQDs was investigated. As shown in Fig. 2B, a typical excitation-dependent behavior was displayed by the fluorescence spectra of NS-CQDs with various excitation wavelengths (340–440 nm). With the increase in excitation wavelength, the emission wavelength was red-shifted. The fluorescence emission intensity gradually increased as the excitation wavelength increased from 340 to 390 nm and then gradually decreased as it further increased from 390 to 440 nm. The behavior could be attributed to various surface states and particle sizes [37]. The fluorescence quantum yield was estimated at 24.1%, making the prepared NS-CQDs an advantageous choice for constructing fluorescent sensing systems with potential applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: XPS and FT-IR analysis of NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to investigate the bonding environment and chemical states of NS-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the significant peaks associated with different elements were observed: S 2p at 164.9 eV, C 1s at 286.3 eV, N 1s at 402.5 eV, and O 1s at 531.6 eV [38]. The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p (Fig. 3B) exhibited two Gaussian peaks at 169.2 and 170.3 eV, indicating the presence of sulfonate (S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2) [39]. Fig. 3C displayed the high-resolution XPS spectrum of C 1s, revealing four peaks at 285.0 eV (C–C/C=C), 286.7 eV (C–OH/C=N/C–SO3–C), 288.0 eV (C=O), and 289.5 eV (COOH bonds). Additionally, the high-resolution N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3D) exhibited three peaks at 400.6 eV (pyridinic N), 401.5 eV (pyrrolic N), and 402.5 eV (graphitic N) [40]. Furthermore, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 3E) displayed three peaks at 532.2 eV (COOH/C-SO3-C), 533.3 eV (C-OH), and 534.3 eV (COOH), elucidating the various chemical states of oxygen in NS-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 3F, the spectral analysis revealed that NS-CQDs were made up of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur with atomic percentages of 64.34%, 27.32%, 5.28%, and 3.06%, respectively. The XPS characterization provided crucial insights into the chemical composition and bonding states of NS-CQDs, essential for understanding their optical and surface properties.

In the study of NS-CQDs, the FT-IR spectrum revealed valuable information about the presence of different functional groups (Fig. 4 ). Notable peaks at 2818 cm−1, 1578 cm−1, and 1123 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of C–H, C=C, and C–C, respectively. The feature peaks centered at 3290 cm−1, 1652 cm−1, and 970 cm−1 were attributed to the O–H, C=O, and C–O–C stretching vibrations, respectively. Moreover, two distinct absorption peaks at 2950 cm−1 and 1446 cm−1 were identified as N–H and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. The appearance of characteristic peaks at 1272 cm−1 and 756 cm−1 confirmed the presence of C–S–C and C–S stretching vibrations, indicating successful N and S doping into the NS-CQDs. The doping effects were crucial in enhancing the fluorescence performance and imparting unique sensing ability to NS-CQDs, making them promising materials for diverse applications.

The stability of fluorescence intensity was an essential factor in determining whether the nanomaterials could be applied to sensing systems. Therefore, this study investigated light exposure time, storage time, and salt concentration as factors affecting NS-CQDs luminescence. Results revealed impressive stability properties for the proposed NS-CQDs fluorescent probe. Even after 60 min of continuous xenon lamp illumination (light exposure), the fluorescence intensity remained virtually unchanged, as depicted in Fig. 5 A. Furthermore, the storage time test demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs was barely affected, even after one week of storage at 4 ℃ (Fig. 5B). Another critical aspect was the salt tolerance of NS-CQDs, assessed in Fig. 5C. Notably, the fluorescence intensity remained constant within NaCl solutions ranging from 0−1.0 M. In conclusion, the synthesized NS-CQDs were a promising fluorescent probe due to their high stability and excellent long-term preservation performance.

The synthesized NS-CQDs fluorescent probe exhibited a maximum fluorescence emission wavelength of 467 nm when excited at 390 nm. At this point, the fluorescent signal of NS-CQDs was in an “ON” state. However, upon adding Cu2+ to the sensing system, the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was noticeably quenched, resulting in an “OFF” state for the fluorescent signal. Subsequently, the addition of GSH to the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ solution led to the restoration of the fluorescent signal to an “ON” state. As a result, an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensing system was successfully constructed based on NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH, as depicted in Fig. 6 .

The fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs was affected by various factors, such as the type of buffer solution used, the pH of the buffer solution, and the reaction time between fluorophores and analyte. Understanding and optimizing these factors was crucial in developing an effective fluorescence sensing system. To determine the most suitable buffer solution for NS-CQDs, six different buffer solutions were tested: phosphoric acid (PB), phosphate (PBS), KH2PO4-NaOH (PDP-NaOH), glycine-NaOH (Gly-NaOH), Tris-HCl, and Britton-Robinson. Among these, PDP-NaOH at pH = 7 emerged as the best choice, as it resulted in the highest fluorescence intensity for NS-CQDs at the emission wavelength of 467 nm (Fig. 7 A). Fig. 7B illustrates the correlation between the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs at 467 nm and the pH level in a 0.1 M PDP-NaOH buffer solution. It could be clearly observed that the highest fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs occurred at pH = 4.5, but the acidic pH was not conducive to constructing biosensing systems. As the pH gradually increased from 4 to 9, the corresponding fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs basically tended to stabilize after pH = 7. Therefore, the buffer medium with pH = 7 was selected in the subsequent experiment.

Another critical aspect investigated was the reaction time between fluorophores and specific analytes. For the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ sensing system, it was found that the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually as the reaction time increased from 0 to 3 min (Fig. 7C). After 3 min, the fluorescence intensity reached a plateau, indicating that 3 min was the optimal reaction time for this system. Similarly, the reaction time between NS-CQDs/Cu2+ and GSH was studied. As shown in Fig. 7D, the maximum fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH system was obtained at 2 min. Consequently, 2 min was chosen as the optimal reaction time for the sensing system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cu2+ by the NS-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of NS-CQDs was examined when exposed to different metal ions (Na+, K+, Ag+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Hg2+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Cr3+) at a concentration of 1 mM. The fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs at 467 nm was recorded under 390 nm excitation. As shown in Fig. 8 A, it was evident that Cu2+ displayed the lowest F/F0 value among the tested metal ions, indicating its potent ability to quench the fluorescence of NS-CQDs. Here, F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of metal ion, respectively. To further evaluate the anti-interference property, 500 μL (1 mM) of various metal ion interfering substances were introduced to the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ sensing system, and the fluorescence intensity was measured. Remarkably, the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system remained almost constant despite the presence of interfering metal ions (Fig. 8B). These results indicated that the established NS-CQDs/Cu2+ sensing system exhibited high selectivity and excellent anti-interference ability.

The synthesized NS-CQDs have emerged as a promising candidate for Cu2+ detection. The ability of NS-CQDs to sense Cu2+ was evaluated by investigating the changes in the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs in response to different concentrations of Cu2+. The concentration of Cu2+ in the experiment was 0.1, 15.6, 31.2, 46.4, 62.8, 77.7, 94.2, 108.5, 124.8, 139.5, 155.5, 169.7, 186.1, and 200.0 μM, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 8C, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased as Cu2+ concentration increased from 0.1 to 200 μM. Notably, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F0 − F/F0) exhibited a robust linear relationship with the concentration of Cu2+ (Fig. 8D). The fitted linear equations, Y = 0.0037 [Cu2+] + 0.0098 (R2 = 0.9977), enabled accurate quantification of Cu2+ concentration. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) using the formula L = 3δ/K (n = 10) was determined to be 0.07 μM (δ: the standard deviation of fluorescence intensity of blank measurements; K: the slope of the calibration curve). Compared with other reported sensors for Cu2+ detection (Table S1), the proposed NS-CQDs fluorescence sensor displayed excellent performance with a wider linear range and lower LOD. These findings highlighted the NS-CQDs' potential as a reliable and sensitive tool for Cu2+ detection in various applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GSH by the NS-CQDs/Cu2+
ce:section.ce:para: A novel fluorescence sensing platform, NS-CQDs/Cu2+, has been constructed by combining 30 μL of NS-CQDs (1.3 mg·mL−1), 50 μL of Cu2+ (200 μM), and 2 mL of PDP-NaOH (0.1 M, pH = 7). The platform's selective response was investigated upon the addition of 100 μL various analytes (1 mM) such as common amino acids, KCl, NaCl, Na2CO3, NaHCO3, ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), salicylic acid (SA), glucose (Glu), maltose (Mal), sucrose (Suc), thioethylamine (TEA), thioglycolic acid (TGA), oxidized glutathione (GSSG), and glutathione (GSH). As depicted in Fig. 9 A, the F1/F values of the corresponding NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system remained basically consistent when various analytes were introduced, and only the F1/F value of GSH was significantly increased. This observation indicated that only GSH could exhibit a “TURN-ON” fluorescence response for the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system. In this context, F and F1 represented the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the analyte, respectively. To evaluate the anti-interference of the NS-CQDs/Cu2+-GSH system, competitive experiments were conducted with various analytes, as shown in Fig. 9B. NS-CQDs/Cu2+ was treated with GSH (1 mM) in the presence of other interfering analytes (1 mM). The changes in fluorescence intensity corresponding to the presence of various interfering analytes were recorded at 467 nm wavelength. It was seen from the figure that the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ platform was decidedly choosy for GSH detection, even in the presence of other interfering substances. In conclusion, the platform's remarkable selectivity and anti-interference capability positioned it as a promising candidate for accurately detecting glutathione (GSH) in complex samples.

The analytical performance of NS-CQDs/Cu2+ towards GSH detection was evaluated by adding various concentrations of GSH. The concentration of GSH in the experiment was 0.6, 11.2, 21.7, 32.4, 42.9, 53.6, 64.0, 74.6, 85.1, 95.6, 106.2, 116.7, 127.4, 138.0, 148.5, 159.0, 169.2, and 180.0 μM, respectively. As shown in Fig. 9C, a significant increase in fluorescence intensity was observed with increasing GSH concentration from 0.6 to 180.0 μM. By analyzing the relationship between (F1-F)/F and GSH concentration in the range of 0.6∼180.0 μM, a fitted linear equation was obtained (Fig. 9D). The linear equation was Y = 0.015 [GSH] + 0.1752, with an R2 value of 0.9973, indicating an excellent linear relationship. Furthermore, the detection limit for GSH was calculated to be 0.1 μM according to L = 3δ/K. These findings indicated that the proposed NS-CQDs/Cu2+ fluorescence sensing platform could sensitively detect GSH. Comparative analysis against other sensors for GSH detection revealed that the proposed NS-CQDs/Cu2+ sensor exhibited a more comprehensive detection range and lower detection limit (Table S2). The above results indicated that the fluorescent sensing platform could be applied to detecting GSH in complex environments. Moreover, it also had a potential application prospect in the long run.

The operating mechanism of the proposed “ON-OFF-ON” sensing system was investigated using various analytical techniques, including transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, and fluorescence lifetime measurements. Initially, the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NS-CQDs by Cu2+ was investigated using TEM. As shown in Fig. 10 A, TEM analysis revealed larger particle size and aggregation in the mixture of NS-CQDs and Cu2+, suggesting possible complexation or electron transfer between them. Next, TEM was also applied to explore the fluorescence restoration mechanism of NS-CQDs/Cu2+ by GSH. Fig. 10B revealed the particle dispersion phenomenon, indicating that GSH separated Cu2+ from the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system, enabling the restoration of NS-CQDs' fluorescence. To further explore the mechanism of the sensing system, the quenching process, UV–vis absorption spectrum, and fluorescence lifetime were measured. It can be seen from Fig. 10C that the Stern-Volmer plot exhibited a nonlinear trend, suggesting that the quenching process of NS-CQDs was non-kinetic, implying a static interaction between NS-CQDs and Cu2+. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of NS-CQDs/Cu2+ exhibited a significant blue-shift and increased absorbance (Fig. 10D), indicating the formation of the ground state complex (NS-CQDs/Cu2+). Interestingly, the fluorescence lifetime of NS-CQDs remains almost unchanged after adding Cu2+ (Fig. 10E), further demonstrating that the quenching occurs through complex formation (static quenching) instead of collisional deactivation (dynamic quenching). Subsequently, upon introducing GSH into the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ system, the Cu2+-GSH complex was formed through a strong coordination interaction between Cu2+ and GSH [41]. Consequently, this process resulted in the reduction of Cu2+ on the surface of NS-CQDs, restoring the NS-CQDs' fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 10D, the UV–vis absorption spectra further investigated the coordination interaction. The formation of the complex (Cu2+/GSH) resulted in the energy reduction of the entire NS-CDs/Cu2+ system, implying potential n → π* and π → π* electronic transitions [42]. Consequently, the UV absorption peak of NS-CDs/Cu2+-GSH exhibited a significant red-shift, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of NS-CQDs was restored. In conclusion, the fluorescence of NS-CQDs was statically quenched by Cu2+ through the formation of the ground state complex (NS-CQDs/Cu2+). As a result, the fluorescence signal switched from “ON” to “OFF”. However, the strong coordination between Cu2+ and GSH caused the NS-CQDs/Cu2+ complex dissociation. This result indicated that the fluorescence of NS-CQDs was restored and the fluorescence signal from “OFF” back to “ON”. Therefore, an “ON-OFF-ON” fluorescent sensing system based on NS-CQDs was successfully constructed in this study.

In short, a high-efficiency fluorescent nanoprobe, NS-CQDs, was synthesized by a dehydration exothermic carbonization method using glucose, ethylenediamine, and concentrated sulfuric acid as raw materials. The synthesized NS-CQDs had an average particle size of 3.3 nm and exhibited remarkable fluorescence stability and excitation wavelength dependence. Further investigations revealed the selective quenching of NS-CQDs' fluorescence by Cu2+ due to the formation of a ground-state complex (NS-CQDs/Cu2+), leading to a static quenching effect. Moreover, the strong coordination interaction between Cu2+ and GSH allowed GSH to restore the quenched fluorescence of NS-CQDs effectively. Therefore, an “ON-OFF-ON” type fluorescent sensor based on NS-CQDs was designed for the sequential detection of Cu2+ and GSH. Furthermore, the sensing conditions of the switch-type fluorescent sensor were optimized, and its selectivity and anti-interference ability were studied. Comparing with other sensors, the switch-type sensor exhibited good selectivity, high sensitivity, and excellent anti-interference. Most importantly, using the spike recovery method, this study effectively evaluated the sensor for quantifying Cu2+ and GSH in human serum samples. Satisfyingly, the fluorescent sensor could reliably monitor the levels of Cu2+ and GSH in human serum samples, indicating its significant application prospects in safeguarding human health.

Huan Wang: Reviewing, Validation. Wencheng Mu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Data curation, Investigation. Siying Wang: Supervision, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Resources. Lin Shi: Reviewing. Tianfeng Ma: Supervision. Yongchang Lu: Formal analysis, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of Qinghai Province, China (Grant No.2019-ZJ-944Q); the Phytochemistry Key Laboratory of Tibetan Plateau of Qinghai Province, China; the Provincial Thousand Talents Program of High-level Innovation Talent, China; the Key Laboratory of Protection, Development and Utilization of Tibetan Medicine Resources on Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, State Ethnic Affairs Commission, China; the Qinghai Minzu University Postgraduate Innovation Project, China; the University-level Planning Project of Qinghai Nationality University of Qinghai Province, China (Grant No.2021XJGH16); the Innovation Team of Medicinal Material Resource Protection and High Value Utilization in Qinghai Province, China. (Grant No.2021XJPI02).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123460.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
